items.q: Which has the longer wavelength: microwaves or radio waves?
items.correct: Radio waves — they sit at the longest-wavelength end of the EM spectrum.
items.misconception: Microwaves, because they are used in 'big' devices like radar.
items.random_wrong: They have the same wavelength because both are used for communication.
items.why: Students remember 'micro' as 'small' and assume the wavelength is the smallest, missing that 'micro' refers historically to the wavelength relative to radio, not to absolute size.

items.q: If a car doubles its speed, what happens to its kinetic energy?
items.correct: It quadruples (because KE = 1/2 mv^2 — kinetic energy is proportional to v^2).
items.misconception: Kinetic energy doubles because energy is proportional to speed.
items.random_wrong: Kinetic energy stays the same because mass hasn't changed.
items.why: Students assume linear relationships (double the input, double the output) and forget the v^2 factor in the kinetic energy formula.
It's Gravity. “Friction” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What unit is force measured in?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Newtons. “Kilograms” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A bag has a mass of 5 kg. On Earth, gravity is 10 N/kg. What is its weight in newtons?
items.why: The real figure is 50. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
